critic_raw: This fMRI clustering vs. cognitive tests experiment has a critical preprocessing leakage issue: functional connectivity matrix computation and dimensionality reduction (PCA/ICA) are 'performed using statistics computed from the entire dataset before splitting.' Functional connectivity matrices summarize BOLD timeseries correlations, and computing these on the combined dataset means that the covariance structure of test-set scans informs the principal components or ICA mixing matrix applied to training data. This is genuine leakage — not just a normalization stability issue but a structural leakage of test-set variance patterns. Additionally, the k-means clustering number k is selected via silhouette score on the validation set — this is correct in isolation, but the cluster assignments derived from k-means trained on the full pre-split data will already reflect test-set structure, making the silhouette-based k selection on validation data a noisy proxy. Feature standardization is also computed on the full dataset. The design also uses paired-sample t-tests or McNemar tests as the stated significance test for the primary comparison — paired t-tests are inappropriate for comparing classification models on a held-out test set (they assume paired continuous differences, while model performance is a single aggregate metric).
all_issues_raised: Functional connectivity computation and PCA/ICA fit on the full dataset before splitting — structural leakage of test-set BOLD covariance structure into dimensionality reduction used for training.
K-means trained on pre-split data; silhouette-based k selection on validation does not correct the structural contamination from full-dataset clustering.
Paired t-test described as the significance test for held-out test set model comparison — this is methodologically incorrect; McNemar test is appropriate for classification comparison.

round_transcripts.critic: The functional connectivity and dimensionality reduction leakage is more serious than simple normalization leakage — it directly shapes the feature space used for training. The statistical test selection issue (paired t-test vs. McNemar) is also a real methodological error.
round_transcripts.defender: On functional connectivity leakage: for neuroimaging studies, population-level connectivity atlases are commonly pre-computed on the full available dataset because individual-level estimates are too noisy. The Atlas (Schaefer 400 ROI parcellation) defines which BOLD timeseries are correlated, not which direction the correlation goes — the connectivity matrix is computed per-scan, not across subjects. The PCA/ICA dimensionality reduction, however, is computed across subjects and does introduce subject-level leakage. On statistical tests: the McNemar test is more appropriate for comparing two classifiers on the same test set — this should be corrected. The paired t-test language is an error in the design.

round_transcripts.critic: The PCA/ICA leakage is conceded. The statistical test error is acknowledged. The functional connectivity per-scan computation is less problematic if it uses a fixed atlas (no cross-subject leakage), but the dimensionality reduction across subjects on the full dataset remains a real issue. The combination of leakage in the dimensionality reduction and the k-means clustering step means the unsupervised features are shaped by test-set participants.
round_transcripts.defender: The dimensionality reduction leakage (PCA/ICA across subjects) should be corrected to use training-set-only statistics. The McNemar test should replace the paired t-test. These corrections are technically straightforward and do not require a fundamental redesign of the study.

all_issues_adjudicated: Cross-subject PCA/ICA fit on full dataset before splitting introduces structural leakage of test-set neuroimaging patterns into feature space — conceded.
Statistical test (paired t-test) is inappropriate for held-out classification model comparison — should use McNemar test.
